states.pageAngle: Georgia is a strong early state because the homeowner guidance clearly ties size to bedroom count and explicitly says garbage disposals require a 50 percent larger tank.
states.ruleSummaryPlainEnglish: Georgia's homeowner guide says the number of bedrooms determines the septic tank size for a single-family residence. It also says the minimum approved tank size is 1,000 gallons and that homes with garbage disposals need a tank that is 50 percent larger.
states.garbageDisposalPolicy: Georgia's homeowner guide says a house with a garbage disposal is required to have a septic tank 50 percent larger than the same house without one.
states.permitSummary: Georgia's onsite sewage program routes homeowners through the county health department. The county environmental health office handles site review, permitting, and inspection in practical terms.
permitPathSteps: Start with the county environmental health office instead of assuming a statewide permit path is enough.
Expect a soil analysis before a septic permit is issued.
Use county inspection and approved contractor resources as the operational path after planning.
states.siteEvalSummary: Georgia's homeowner guidance says all properties must have a soil analysis and that site conditions like water table and limiting layer depth affect the usable drainfield area.
ruleHighlights: The guide says the number of bedrooms determines the septic tank size for a single-family residence.
Peak daily water usage is based on 150 gallons per bedroom.
The minimum approved tank size is 1,000 gallons, which the guide says serves a 3- or 4-bedroom house.
A garbage disposal requires a septic tank that is 50 percent larger.
states.localOverrideNote: County health department implementation and site conditions can still change the final system recommendation even when the homeowner guide examples look straightforward.
itemsNeedingVerification: Pull the current rule-text citation behind the homeowner guide's disposal upsizing language.
Decide how aggressively the public calculator should use the 150 gallons-per-bedroom design-flow anchor.

states.pageAngle: Pennsylvania is valuable because DEP gives homeowner-facing size language while the actual permit path runs through municipalities and Sewage Enforcement Officers, which creates a strong unique trust angle.
states.ruleSummaryPlainEnglish: Pennsylvania DEP says septic tank sizes are generally based on the number of bedrooms and uses 900 gallons or larger for a three-bedroom home as the public homeowner example. DEP also makes the local municipal and Sewage Enforcement Officer role very clear.
states.garbageDisposalPolicy: DEP's homeowner page advises sparse use of garbage disposals because they place a greater burden on the septic system, but no direct disposal size multiplier has been verified from the current public source set.
states.permitSummary: Pennsylvania's practical permit path is local. DEP points homeowners to the municipality or local agency that administers on-lot sewage rules and to the Sewage Enforcement Officer for permitting and enforcement activities.
permitPathSteps: Find the municipality or local agency responsible for on-lot sewage permitting before assuming the state page is enough.
Work through the local Sewage Enforcement Officer for permits and enforcement steps.
Use DEP's homeowner guidance for maintenance and sizing context, but expect local agency process details to control the next step.
states.siteEvalSummary: DEP emphasizes that septic systems work only if the site has suitable soil conditions. That makes site and field conditions a major reason to keep homeowner estimates wide.
ruleHighlights: DEP says septic tank sizes are generally based on the number of bedrooms in the home.
DEP gives 900 gallons or larger as the homeowner-facing size example for a three-bedroom house.
DEP says garbage disposal use should be sparse because it places a greater burden on the system.
DEP says larger households may need pumping more often than the typical three- to five-year interval.
states.localOverrideNote: Municipal administration and Sewage Enforcement Officer decisions can change how a Pennsylvania homeowner actually moves from estimate to permit.
itemsNeedingVerification: Find an additional official source that publishes a fuller bedroom-to-capacity table instead of only the three-bedroom example.
Document whether specific municipal programs surface homeowner-facing replacement sequencing that is worth publishing.

states.pageAngle: Connecticut can be genuinely differentiated because DPH uses design sewage flow, potential bedrooms, and code-complying area rules that national generic pages usually fail to explain well.
states.ruleSummaryPlainEnglish: Connecticut DPH says residential sewage disposal sizing is based on bedroom count, not actual occupant count, and uses 150 gallons per bedroom. The state also says new homes are based on potential bedrooms and that additions can trigger code-complying area review and soil testing.
states.garbageDisposalPolicy: No direct public disposal multiplier has been confirmed from the current Connecticut source set.
states.permitSummary: For systems under 5,000 gallons per day, the local director of health or approved agent reviews the site investigation and issues the approval to construct. After construction, inspection, and as-built review, the same local authority issues the permit to discharge.
permitPathSteps: Treat the local director of health or approved agent as the practical permit authority for most residential systems.
Expect a site investigation and soil testing before an approval to construct is granted.
After inspection and as-built documentation, expect a permit to discharge before use.
states.siteEvalSummary: Connecticut's code-complying area rules make room additions and change-in-use scenarios especially important because the available reserve area and soil conditions affect whether a property still works under the public health code.
ruleHighlights: Connecticut says residential sewage disposal systems are based on bedroom count, not the number of people living in the house.
DPH uses 150 gallons per bedroom for residential design sewage flow.
New homes are sized on potential bedrooms, not just currently labeled bedrooms.
Building additions and changes in use can require code-complying area review and soil testing.
states.localOverrideNote: Local health officials and approved agents have a direct role in site review, construction approval, and final discharge permitting, so a Connecticut homeowner should expect strong local implementation.
itemsNeedingVerification: Extract a homeowner-safe tank sizing bridge from design flow to public gallon recommendations.
Document whether any local health district patterns deserve special publishing caveats.

states.pageAngle: Oregon is a strong organic wedge because the real homeowner story is permit sequencing and site evaluation, not fake tank precision. That creates a page national cost sites usually cannot explain well.
states.ruleSummaryPlainEnglish: Oregon puts site evaluation before septic permitting. DEQ says a site evaluation does not guarantee approval of any particular septic system type, and homeowners may also need an authorization notice when changing use, increasing sewage flow, or connecting an ADU to an existing system.
states.garbageDisposalPolicy: No direct public disposal multiplier has been verified from the current Oregon source set.
states.permitSummary: Oregon requires a septic permit to install, alter, or repair a system, and the permit is valid for one year after issuance. In most counties, homeowners work with the local septic permitting authority rather than DEQ directly.
permitPathSteps: Start with site evaluation rather than assuming a generic install estimate is enough.
If changing use, increasing sewage flow, or connecting an ADU, check whether an authorization notice applies before the permit step.
After the site and application materials are ready, move into the septic permit process with the local permitting authority.
states.siteEvalSummary: DEQ says a site evaluation reviews soil test pits in both the proposed initial and replacement soil absorption areas. It also says the evaluation does not guarantee that any particular septic system will be approved.
ruleHighlights: A site evaluation reviews soil conditions in both the initial and replacement soil absorption areas.
DEQ says a site evaluation does not guarantee approval of any specific septic system type.
An authorization notice may be required for a change in use, increased sewage flow, or ADU connection to an existing system.
A septic permit is required to install, alter, or repair a system and is valid for one year.
states.localOverrideNote: Oregon's local permitting structure matters because most counties work through local onsite programs even though DEQ sets the statewide program frame.
itemsNeedingVerification: Find a homeowner-safe way to map Oregon's site-evaluation-first process into calculator confidence bands.
Document whether Oregon publishes any public-facing residential sizing examples worth surfacing.

states.agencyName: Missouri Department of Health and Senior Services

states.ruleSummaryPlainEnglish: Missouri has a statewide onsite wastewater program, but the homeowner-safe sizing logic still needs deeper extraction from official materials.
states.permitSummary: Local authority context is important and should be surfaced on the state page.
states.siteEvalSummary: System type and approval path depend on local review and site conditions.
states.localOverrideNote: Local authorities may control parts of the review and permitting process.
itemsNeedingVerification: Extract residential sizing logic from a higher-detail official source.

states.ruleSummaryPlainEnglish: North Carolina has a dedicated onsite wastewater branch, but public-facing homeowner sizing logic still needs deeper extraction.
states.permitSummary: Permitting and site review are handled within the onsite wastewater program framework.
states.siteEvalSummary: Site conditions remain a major driver of system class and cost.

states.permitSummary: Local health jurisdictions are central to practical permitting and review.
states.siteEvalSummary: Site-specific review affects the likely system and final quote range.

states.pageAngle: Massachusetts has a strong Title 5 angle, but the homeowner-safe design-flow summary still needs extraction before it becomes an anchor state.
states.ruleSummaryPlainEnglish: Massachusetts Title 5 is a strong homeowner-facing angle, but the publishable design-flow summary still needs deeper extraction.
states.permitSummary: Title 5 creates a distinct homeowner and home-sale context that should shape content and CTA placement.
states.siteEvalSummary: Inspection and property-transfer context are especially important in Massachusetts.
states.localOverrideNote: Local boards and property-specific conditions can affect the final path under Title 5.

states.ruleSummaryPlainEnglish: Florida has a statewide OSTDS program and strong homeowner relevance, but detailed sizing and site-condition logic still need extraction.
states.permitSummary: State program guidance exists, but local and site-specific process details still need clearer mapping.
states.siteEvalSummary: Water table and site conditions are likely to be major cost drivers in Florida content.
